meta.title: SG-SCD-1: Workbooks, Datasets, Embedding and Performance

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of a Sigma workbook?

options.text: A collaborative analytical document containing pages and data elements
options.explanation: A Sigma workbook is the core collaborative surface for analysis and presentation — it contains pages, charts, tables, and controls that users interact with.

options.text: A standalone connection configuration for warehouse credentials
options.explanation: Connection configurations are managed in the Connections section of Administration, not in workbooks.

options.text: A published dataset schema that enforces column-level governance
options.explanation: Published datasets serve as the governed semantic layer; a workbook is the analytical document built on top of such datasets.

options.text: A scheduled export job that sends reports to email recipients
options.explanation: Scheduled exports are a workbook feature, not the primary purpose of the workbook itself.

questions.stem: A team wants one date selector to filter multiple workbook elements at once. What should they configure?

options.text: A materialization schedule tied to the date column
options.explanation: Materialization schedules persist cached results on a time basis and are unrelated to interactive filtering.

options.text: A control target linking the date control to each element
options.explanation: Controls in Sigma can be configured with targets that specify which workbook elements they filter, allowing one date selector to drive multiple charts and tables simultaneously.

options.text: A user attribute that maps the date to a warehouse role
options.explanation: User attributes are used for row-level security and personalization, not for wiring UI controls to elements.

options.text: A JWT claim embedding the date range in the signed URL
options.explanation: JWT claims are used for authorizing embedded access, not for cross-element filtering within a workbook.

questions.stem: Why would a team publish a Sigma dataset?

options.text: To bypass the warehouse and store data inside Sigma's own storage layer
options.explanation: Sigma always queries the underlying cloud data warehouse; it does not maintain its own data storage layer.

options.text: To convert every workbook element into a REST API endpoint
options.explanation: Datasets are analytical models, not REST endpoints. The Sigma API provides programmatic access but is separate from datasets.

options.text: To restrict access to only Admin-role users when opening the workbook
options.explanation: Dataset publishing is about reuse and governance, not about restricting workbook access to admins.

options.text: To create a reusable governed semantic layer that many workbooks can build on
options.explanation: Published datasets define shared business logic — joins, column definitions, metrics — that multiple workbooks can reference, improving consistency and governance across the organization.

questions.stem: What is the main advantage of Sigma's visual join builder?

options.text: It physically denormalizes and materializes all joined tables in the warehouse automatically
options.explanation: Sigma does not force physical denormalization or automatic materialization; the join is computed at query time unless the developer explicitly schedules materialization.

options.text: It generates a published dataset from the join and enforces column-level encryption
options.explanation: The join builder does not encrypt columns; column-level security is managed through permission settings and user attributes.

options.text: It replaces workbook permission controls with row-level security based on join conditions
options.explanation: Permissions are managed separately from join logic; join conditions define data relationships, not access control.

options.text: It lets users define join types and key conditions without hand-writing SQL
options.explanation: The visual join builder presents join configuration — join type, key columns, and join conditions — as a graphical interface, making it accessible to users who are not comfortable writing SQL while still generating efficient warehouse-native queries.

questions.stem: Which Sigma feature supports manual business-user writeback to the connected warehouse?

options.text: Input tables
options.explanation: Input tables are designed specifically for governed manual data entry and writeback use cases, allowing business users to type values that are persisted back to the warehouse.

options.text: Workbook controls with a list-value source
options.explanation: Workbook controls — such as dropdowns or date pickers — filter or parameterize the analytical view; they do not write new records to the warehouse.

options.text: Version-tagged workbook templates
options.explanation: Version tags track content milestones for rollback and audit, not for data writeback.

options.text: Scheduled materialization jobs
options.explanation: Materialization schedules cache existing query results as warehouse tables; they do not accept user-entered data.

questions.stem: When is materialization most useful in Sigma?

options.text: When you want to share a workbook with users who do not have warehouse credentials
options.explanation: Access to workbook data is governed by Sigma permissions and warehouse roles, not by materialization; users without warehouse credentials cannot bypass that through materialization.

options.text: When repeated workbook queries are expensive and a cached warehouse table will improve response time
options.explanation: Materialization persists expensive intermediate query results as warehouse tables on a schedule, reducing the compute cost and improving response time for workbooks that repeatedly run the same heavy query.

options.text: When you need to enable input table writeback for business users
options.explanation: Input table writeback is a distinct feature that allows data entry; it does not depend on materialization being enabled.

options.text: When a control needs to filter multiple elements simultaneously
options.explanation: Cross-element filtering is accomplished using control targets, not materialization schedules.

questions.stem: Which statement best describes Sigma query execution?

options.text: Sigma exports data to a local browser cache and runs all computations client-side in JavaScript
options.explanation: Sigma does not copy full datasets to the browser; only result sets are returned to the UI after warehouse execution.

options.text: Sigma requires users to write and submit raw SQL for every workbook element
options.explanation: Sigma's value is that users can build analyses without writing SQL; the platform generates SQL automatically.

options.text: Sigma compiles workbook operations into warehouse-native SQL and pushes execution to the connected warehouse
options.explanation: Sigma's core design principle is query pushdown — it translates workbook interactions into SQL that executes directly on the connected warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, etc.), leveraging the warehouse's compute and scale.

options.text: Sigma runs a proprietary in-memory engine inside the Sigma cloud and never touches the warehouse after initial load
options.explanation: Sigma does not maintain a proprietary in-memory engine that replaces the warehouse; the warehouse is the execution engine.

questions.stem: A workbook is slow because a massive join happens before any filters are applied. What is the best first optimization?

options.text: Enable materialization with a daily schedule so the joined data is pre-cached
options.explanation: Materialization caches the result of the existing expensive query; it does not restructure the query to avoid the expensive join. It can help for repeated reads but does not fix the underlying inefficiency.

options.text: Split the join into multiple workbook pages to distribute the load
options.explanation: Splitting the join across workbook pages does not change the SQL generated for any individual element; each page would still join the full dataset.

options.text: Convert the join to a published dataset to reduce per-workbook join overhead
options.explanation: Publishing to a dataset moves the join definition but does not add a filter; the same expensive full-scan join would still execute at query time.

options.text: Apply the filter before the join to reduce row volume entering the join operation
options.explanation: Reducing the number of rows before the join significantly lowers the amount of data the warehouse must process. Applying selective filters early is the most impactful first step because it directly shrinks join input size and enables better pushdown.

questions.stem: Which Sigma function category is most appropriate for expressions such as `DateAdd` and `DateDiff`?

options.text: Date and time functions
options.explanation: `DateAdd` and `DateDiff` operate on date and time values — adding intervals or computing the difference between two dates — which places them squarely in the date and time function category.

options.text: Lookup and relationship functions
options.explanation: Lookup functions retrieve matching values from another element or dataset based on a key; they do not manipulate date values.

options.text: Aggregate functions
options.explanation: Aggregate functions like `Sum`, `Count`, or `Avg` reduce many rows to a single value; date arithmetic operates at the row level.

options.text: Conditional functions
options.explanation: Conditional functions like `If` and `Coalesce` evaluate logical expressions; `DateAdd` and `DateDiff` are not conditional in nature.

questions.stem: What problem does the `Lookup` function primarily solve in Sigma?

options.text: Running a window calculation such as cumulative sum or rolling average across a result set
options.explanation: Running totals and rolling averages are handled by window functions such as `RunningSum` or `MovingAvg`, not by `Lookup`.

options.text: Retrieving a related value from another element or result set based on a matching key
options.explanation: The `Lookup` function performs a key-based value retrieval from another element — similar to a VLOOKUP in spreadsheets — allowing a column in one element to pull in a matching value from a different data source or result set.

options.text: Counting distinct values across multiple columns in the same table
options.explanation: Counting distinct values uses aggregate functions like `CountDistinct`; `Lookup` does not aggregate.

options.text: Replacing null values with a fallback from a separate data source
options.explanation: Null replacement is handled by conditional functions like `Coalesce` or `IsNull`; while `Lookup` could return a fallback as a side effect, that is not its primary purpose.

questions.stem: Which element type is most appropriate for showing one headline KPI such as month-to-date revenue?

options.text: A pivot table element with a single measure and no row groups
options.explanation: A pivot table with one measure and no row groups would display the metric but is designed for multi-dimensional cross-tabulation, not headline display.

options.text: A bar chart with a single series and no dimensions
options.explanation: A bar chart with one series and no dimensions would render a single bar — technically showing the value, but charts are designed for comparisons and trends, not single-metric prominence.

options.text: A KPI card element
options.explanation: KPI cards are purpose-built to display a single headline metric prominently, with optional comparison values such as period-over-period change, making them ideal for executive-level summaries.

options.text: A data table filtered to one row
options.explanation: Filtering a full data table to one row carries all the overhead of a table element and does not give the visual prominence a KPI display requires.

questions.stem: Why might a developer link workbooks together?

options.text: To reuse context parameters and enable navigation across related analytical experiences
options.explanation: Workbook links allow a developer to pass filter values or parameters from one workbook to another and create drill-through navigation paths, giving users a connected analytical experience across separate content.

options.text: To merge the two workbooks into one shared dataset automatically
options.explanation: Linking workbooks does not merge them or create shared datasets; the two workbooks remain independent and manage their own data sources.

options.text: To synchronize version history across both workbooks
options.explanation: Version history is tracked independently per workbook; linking does not synchronize versions between them.

options.text: To grant the target workbook access to the source workbook's warehouse connection
options.explanation: Warehouse connections are managed in Administration and are not shared or granted through workbook links.

questions.stem: A user should be able to explore and build ad hoc analyses but not administer the entire organization. Which Sigma role is the closest fit?

options.text: Viewer, because Viewers can explore workbooks and create new ones in their personal space
options.explanation: The Viewer role is focused on consuming published content; Viewers typically cannot create or edit workbooks, which makes it too restrictive for ad hoc analysis.

options.text: Creator or Explorer, because these roles support analysis and content authoring without organization-wide administration
options.explanation: The Creator and Explorer account types allow users to build analyses, create workbooks, and explore data without having the organization-wide administrative capabilities of the Admin role.

options.text: Admin, because ad hoc analysis requires elevated data access permissions
options.explanation: Admin is the most privileged role, with access to organization settings, connections, and user management — far broader than what an analyst needs.

options.text: There is no built-in role for this; a custom role must be created via the API
options.explanation: Sigma provides built-in account types that cover this use case; a custom API-created role is not required.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of user attributes in Sigma security design?

options.text: To define the warehouse connection credentials for each individual user
options.explanation: Warehouse credentials are configured in Connections by Admins and applied at the connection level, not per user via user attributes.

options.text: To configure the appearance and branding of the Sigma interface per team
options.explanation: Sigma's branding and theming is controlled in Organization settings, not through user attributes.

options.text: To support dynamic row-level filtering and tenant personalization based on user identity
options.explanation: User attributes are key-value pairs assigned to users or teams that can be referenced in dataset filters and embed parameters, enabling dynamic row-level security so each user only sees the data relevant to their identity or tenant.

options.text: To set rate limits on API requests made by each user account
options.explanation: API rate limits are not configured through user attributes; they are governed by Sigma's platform policies and the REST API authentication layer.

questions.stem: What does Sigma version history help teams do?

options.text: Schedule materialization runs on a fixed cadence
options.explanation: Materialization schedules are configured separately in the workbook's materialization settings; version history does not control when caching jobs run.

options.text: Sync workbook changes to an external Git repository automatically
options.explanation: Sigma does not natively sync workbook changes to external Git repositories; version history is Sigma's own internal change tracking.

options.text: Generate signed embed tokens for each workbook version
options.explanation: Embed tokens are generated at runtime by the embedding application server; they are not tied to workbook version history entries.

options.text: Review prior workbook states and restore to an earlier version if needed
options.explanation: Version history captures a timeline of workbook edits, allowing teams to review what changed over time and restore the workbook to a previous state if a change introduced errors or regressions.

questions.stem: Which approach is used for client-side embedded Sigma analytics?

options.text: Generate a signed embed URL or JWT on the application server and load it in an iframe
options.explanation: Sigma embedding is based on generating a signed URL (or JWT-based token) server-side with appropriate claims such as user email, team, and user attributes, then rendering it in an iframe within the host application.

options.text: Export the workbook to a static HTML file and host it on the customer's CDN
options.explanation: Sigma workbooks are dynamic, live-querying documents; they cannot be meaningfully exported to static HTML while retaining interactive filtering and real-time data.

options.text: Materialize every workbook page to a Cloud Storage bucket before embedding
options.explanation: Materialization is an optional performance optimization for caching expensive queries; it is not required before embedding and does not produce an embeddable artifact.

options.text: Copy the workbook data into the embedding application's own database first
options.explanation: Sigma always queries the connected warehouse at query time; data is not copied into the embedding application's database.

questions.stem: Why should embed tokens include an expiry?

options.text: Sigma requires an expiry to determine when to refresh the workbook's materialized cache
options.explanation: Materialization scheduling is independent of embed token expiry; the two features are unrelated.

options.text: Token expiry limits the window in which a stolen or intercepted embed URL can be misused
options.explanation: A short-lived expiry (typically seconds to minutes) ensures that even if a signed embed URL is intercepted or leaked, the attacker has only a narrow window before the token becomes invalid and access is denied.

options.text: Without an expiry the embedded workbook cannot load user attributes from the identity provider
options.explanation: User attributes are embedded in the token's claims at generation time; they do not require a live connection to an identity provider at load time, and expiry does not control this flow.

options.text: Sigma's API rate limiter uses token expiry to determine the allowed request quota
options.explanation: API rate limits are governed by Sigma's platform policies based on API keys and organization settings, not by embed token expiry.

questions.stem: Which scenario is a good fit for an input table?

options.text: A sales manager manually enters quarterly targets that should be stored back in the warehouse alongside actuals
options.explanation: Input tables are designed for governed manual data entry and writeback use cases — scenarios where business users need to add records that are persisted back to the warehouse, such as budgets, targets, or annotations.

options.text: A developer wants to pass a tenant identifier through a signed embed URL
options.explanation: Passing tenant context through a signed embed URL uses user attributes and JWT embed parameters, not input tables.

options.text: An analyst wants to cache an expensive aggregation query for reuse
options.explanation: Caching an expensive aggregation query is achieved through materialization, not input tables.

options.text: An admin wants to configure a new warehouse connection for the organization
options.explanation: Warehouse connections are configured in the Connections section of Sigma Administration, not through input tables.

questions.stem: A workbook uses complex calculations across the same billions-row dataset every morning. Which feature is most likely to reduce repeated runtime cost?

options.text: Add a second workbook page that duplicates the calculation for easier review
options.explanation: Duplicating the calculation on a second page doubles the query cost rather than reducing it.

options.text: Convert all numeric measures to text columns to simplify type handling
options.explanation: Converting measures to text changes the data type but does not reduce the rows scanned or the complexity of the underlying computation.

options.text: Schedule a materialization that pre-computes the expensive result and stores it as a warehouse table
options.explanation: Materialization pre-computes and persists the expensive query result as a native warehouse table on a schedule (e.g., overnight). When users open the workbook in the morning, Sigma reads from the cached table instead of re-running the billions-row computation, dramatically reducing cost and latency.

options.text: Publish the workbook to a shared workspace so users run queries simultaneously
options.explanation: Having multiple users run the workbook simultaneously increases the number of warehouse query executions; it does not reduce per-query cost.

questions.stem: What is the difference between workspace permissions and workbook permissions?

options.text: Workspace permissions apply only to scheduled exports while workbook permissions apply to interactive viewing
options.explanation: Workspace permissions control access to all content types within the workspace, not just scheduled exports.

options.text: They are always identical because workbook permissions inherit from the workspace and cannot be overridden
options.explanation: Permissions can be set independently at the workbook level and can differ from the workspace-level grant; they are not always identical.

options.text: Workbook permissions grant broader organization-wide access while workspace permissions are scoped to a single asset
options.explanation: This reverses the relationship — workspace permissions are broader (covering many assets), while workbook permissions are scoped to a single asset.

options.text: Workspace permissions grant access across all content in the workspace, while workbook permissions target specific workbooks with potentially different access levels
options.explanation: Workspace-level permissions apply broadly to all content inside that workspace, while individual workbook permissions allow more granular access control — for example, granting one team Viewer access at the workspace level but Editor access on a specific workbook.

questions.stem: Which calculation type is best for running totals or rankings within Sigma?

options.text: Window functions such as running average or rank
options.explanation: Running totals and rankings are analytical patterns that require computing a value for each row relative to surrounding rows within a defined order or partition — which is exactly what window functions like `RunningSum`, `Rank`, and `CumulativeDistribution` provide.

options.text: Aggregate functions such as Sum and Count applied to the full table
options.explanation: Standard aggregates like `Sum` collapse all rows into a single total; they do not produce per-row running values.

options.text: Lookup functions that retrieve values from another element
options.explanation: Lookup functions retrieve a matching value from another element based on a key; they are not designed for ordered running calculations.

options.text: Conditional functions such as If and Switch applied row by row
options.explanation: Conditional functions evaluate logical expressions and return one value or another; they do not accumulate values across rows in order.

questions.stem: Why should a developer prefer a published dataset over rebuilding the same joins in many workbooks?

options.text: Published datasets remove the need for a warehouse connection and can run offline
options.explanation: Sigma always queries the connected warehouse; published datasets do not bypass the warehouse or enable offline execution.

options.text: A published dataset centralizes business logic, improving governance and ensuring consistent metric definitions across all workbooks
options.explanation: Defining joins, column aliases, and metrics once in a published dataset means every workbook that builds on it shares the same business logic. If the definition changes — such as a revenue metric formula update — it propagates to all dependent workbooks automatically.

options.text: Published datasets automatically materialize their joins on a daily schedule
options.explanation: Materialization is a separate optional feature that must be explicitly configured; publishing a dataset does not automatically schedule materialization.

options.text: Published datasets grant every user who accesses them full Admin permissions on the source tables
options.explanation: Publishing a dataset does not grant users elevated warehouse permissions; access is still governed by Sigma workspace and workbook permissions.

questions.stem: An embedded workbook must apply a tenant-specific filter automatically for each signed-in customer. Which design best supports this?

options.text: Pass user or tenant context through signed embed parameters and apply user attributes to drive row-level filtering in the dataset
options.explanation: Embedding context plus user attributes supports dynamic tenant-aware filtering at scale. The application server encodes the tenant identifier in the signed embed URL; Sigma maps it to a user attribute that drives a dataset filter, so each customer sees only their own data without requiring separate workbooks or materials.

options.text: Create one separate workbook per customer and generate a unique embed URL for each workbook
options.explanation: Maintaining one workbook per customer is operationally unscalable — any change to the workbook layout must be replicated across every customer copy.

options.text: Use a workbook control with a hardcoded list of tenant values that users select manually
options.explanation: A hardcoded control list requires the customer to select their tenant, which is a security risk (they could select another tenant) and poor UX.

options.text: Materialize a separate warehouse table per tenant and point each embed at the appropriate table
options.explanation: Creating and managing a separate materialized table per tenant is highly operationally complex and does not scale as the customer base grows.

questions.stem: Which role is usually most restricted in Sigma?

options.text: Creator
options.explanation: Creators have broader capabilities than Viewers, including the ability to build and publish workbooks.

options.text: Explorer
options.explanation: Explorers can also explore and interact with data more deeply than Viewers, such as editing in their personal Explore space.

options.text: Viewer
options.explanation: The Viewer account type provides the most restricted access — Viewers can consume published workbooks and interact with controls but cannot create, edit, or save workbooks, making it the least privileged of the common user-facing account types.

options.text: Admin
options.explanation: Admin is the most privileged account type, with full control over organization settings, connections, and user management.

questions.stem: Why is query pushdown important in Sigma?

options.text: It removes the need for users to write SQL and generates Python scripts instead
options.explanation: Sigma generates SQL for warehouse execution, not Python scripts; the goal is warehouse-native computation, not replacing SQL with another language.

options.text: It copies the entire source table into Sigma's own cache before any filtering occurs
options.explanation: Sigma does not copy entire tables into its own layer before filtering; predicates are pushed into the warehouse query, reducing data movement.

options.text: It limits the number of concurrent queries a user can run to prevent warehouse overload
options.explanation: Query pushdown refers to where computation happens (warehouse vs. Sigma), not to concurrency throttling.

options.text: It sends computations to the warehouse engine so data is processed close to where it lives, using the warehouse's scale and speed
options.explanation: Pushdown executes SQL directly on the connected warehouse — Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, or Redshift — leveraging that platform's distributed compute, indexes, and caching rather than transferring raw data to Sigma servers for processing.

questions.stem: What is the main tradeoff of materialization?

options.text: Perfect real-time freshness with zero maintenance overhead
options.explanation: Materialization is by definition not real-time; data is only as fresh as the last completed refresh run, so freshness and zero maintenance cannot both be guaranteed.

options.text: Faster repeated reads at the cost of managing refresh schedules and accepting some data staleness between runs
options.explanation: Materialized results improve query speed and reduce compute costs for repeated reads, but they introduce a staleness window — the cached table reflects the data as of the last scheduled refresh, not the current warehouse state.

options.text: Elimination of all warehouse costs because the data no longer queries the source
options.explanation: Warehouse costs are not eliminated — the materialization job itself consumes warehouse compute when it runs, and the cached table occupies warehouse storage.

options.text: Automatic propagation of updated permissions from the source table to the materialized result
options.explanation: Materialized tables are separate physical objects; permission changes on the source table are not automatically reflected in the materialized result without explicit reconfiguration.

questions.stem: A developer wants a toggle that switches a chart between Gross Margin and Revenue. Which Sigma feature is the best fit?

options.text: A published dataset with two separate metric columns pre-computed
options.explanation: Having two columns in a published dataset does not provide the interactive toggle; the user would see both columns simultaneously rather than switching between them.

options.text: A workbook control — such as a segmented button — whose value drives a conditional calculation in the chart
options.explanation: Controls such as a list, radio button, or segmented button can be configured to drive chart logic through a conditional calculation — for example, `If([Control] = "Gross Margin", [Gross Margin], [Revenue])` — allowing the user to toggle the displayed metric interactively.

options.text: A materialization schedule that alternates between two pre-cached versions of the chart
options.explanation: Materialization schedules cache static snapshots; they cannot alternate between different metric views based on user interaction.

options.text: An input table where the user types the desired metric name each time
options.explanation: Requiring users to type a metric name in an input table is error-prone and a poor UX pattern compared to a dedicated interactive control.

questions.stem: What can the Sigma REST API help automate?

options.text: Programmatic management of workbooks, members, connections, and scheduled exports
options.explanation: The Sigma REST API exposes endpoints for managing workbooks, members, teams, connections, datasets, and scheduled exports — enabling CI/CD pipelines, provisioning scripts, and integration workflows to automate Sigma operations.

options.text: Running SQL queries directly on the connected warehouse from outside Sigma
options.explanation: The Sigma API manages Sigma content and operations; SQL queries are executed through the warehouse connection, not through the Sigma REST API.

options.text: Configuring warehouse cluster sizes and autoscaling policies
options.explanation: Warehouse cluster configuration is managed in the warehouse platform itself (e.g., Snowflake or BigQuery), not through the Sigma API.

options.text: Generating Sigma embed tokens without a signing secret
options.explanation: Embed tokens always require a valid embed client ID and secret for signing; the API does not bypass this security requirement.

questions.stem: A company needs separate visibility by customer account inside the same workbook. Which approach is most scalable?

options.text: Create a separate workbook for each customer account and share it directly with their Sigma users
options.explanation: Maintaining a separate workbook per customer account is operationally unscalable; any workbook update must be repeated across every copy.

options.text: Use a workbook control with a dropdown listing all customer accounts, relying on users to select their own
options.explanation: Relying on users to select their own account from a dropdown is a security risk — a user could select a different account and see another customer's data.

options.text: Use row-level filtering driven by user attributes so each user automatically sees only their account's data
options.explanation: User-attribute-driven row-level security filters data at query time based on the authenticated user's identity, so a single workbook securely serves all customer accounts — each user sees only their account's rows without any manual selection.

options.text: Export the data to CSV per account and distribute via email on a schedule
options.explanation: CSV email distribution is a manual, batch-oriented process that does not provide the interactive, real-time analytical experience the question implies, and does not scale to many accounts.

questions.stem: Which warehouse platforms can Sigma commonly connect to?

options.text: Only Snowflake, because Sigma was originally built exclusively for Snowflake
options.explanation: While Sigma's origins are closely associated with Snowflake, it now supports multiple warehouse platforms and is not limited to Snowflake.

options.text: Only self-hosted databases accessible via a private VPN gateway
options.explanation: Sigma connects to cloud data warehouses via standard drivers and APIs; it is not limited to databases behind private VPN gateways.

options.text: Only cloud platforms that run on AWS; GCP and Azure are not supported
options.explanation: Sigma supports warehouses running on GCP (BigQuery) and Azure (Synapse, Databricks) in addition to AWS-hosted warehouses.

options.text: Cloud data warehouses such as Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, and Databricks
options.explanation: Sigma supports connections to multiple cloud data warehouses and lakehouses including Snowflake, Google BigQuery, Amazon Redshift, and Databricks SQL, giving organizations flexibility in their warehouse choice.

questions.stem: A developer wants to troubleshoot why a workbook calculation is expensive. What is the most useful first step?

options.text: Inspect the generated SQL and query plan to identify filters, joins, or functions that prevent efficient pushdown
options.explanation: Performance troubleshooting starts by understanding the shape of the SQL Sigma generates. Examining the query reveals which operations — such as a cross join, a missing filter push, or a non-sargable function — are causing the warehouse to do unnecessary work.

options.text: Publish the workbook as a template and let Sigma auto-optimize the SQL
options.explanation: Publishing as a template does not trigger any SQL optimization; the same query logic is preserved in the published version.

options.text: Add more user attributes to restrict which rows each user can query
options.explanation: Adding user attributes adds row-level filter predicates, which can reduce scanned data as a side effect, but it is not a diagnostic step and should not be the first move when troubleshooting a specific expensive calculation.

options.text: Convert the workbook element into an input table and re-enter the values manually
options.explanation: Converting a calculation to an input table means replacing a computed result with manually entered data, which defeats the purpose of the calculation and is not a performance fix.

questions.stem: Why might a team tag workbook versions before a release?

options.text: Version tags automatically materialize the workbook's data at the point of tagging
options.explanation: Version tags capture a snapshot of the workbook definition, not the underlying data; they do not trigger materialization or persist query results.

options.text: Version tags mark known-good milestones that can be referenced for rollback, audit, or embedding specific published states
options.explanation: Version tags create named checkpoints in a workbook's history. Teams can tag a version before deploying to ensure they can roll back to a known-good state, and embedded analytics can reference a tagged version to pin the embed to a stable workbook release.

options.text: Version tags enable embed tokens to be generated without a signing secret
options.explanation: Embed tokens always require a valid signing secret; version tags have no bearing on that security requirement.

options.text: Version tags copy the workbook to a separate workspace for QA testing
options.explanation: Version tags do not copy workbooks to another workspace; they are metadata applied to the workbook's history within its current location.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of an embed sandbox or test environment?

options.text: To validate the embedded experience — including token generation, user attribute filtering, and layout — before rolling out to production users
options.explanation: A sandbox or test environment lets developers verify that embed token signing, user attribute injection, dynamic filtering, and iframe rendering all work correctly before exposing the integration to real end users in production.

options.text: To permanently replace the production embed with a read-only view that prevents data changes
options.explanation: A test environment is a temporary validation context, not a permanent production replacement; it does not alter data permissions.

options.text: To automatically generate a signed embed token without requiring a server-side application
options.explanation: Signed embed tokens must always be generated server-side using the embed client secret; a sandbox does not bypass this requirement.

options.text: To convert the embedded workbook into a standalone HTML export for offline distribution
options.explanation: Sigma embeds are live, interactive experiences that query the warehouse in real time; they cannot be converted to static HTML exports.

questions.stem: Which action best reduces workbook latency when users regularly open a page with a highly selective control?

options.text: Materialize the entire unfiltered dataset so the page loads from a cache regardless of the control value
options.explanation: Materializing the full unfiltered dataset does not help when the control is highly selective — the cache still needs to be scanned in full unless the materialization is also partitioned or filtered by the control value.

options.text: Add more visual elements to the page to distribute the rendering workload
options.explanation: Adding more visual elements increases the number of queries Sigma must execute, which adds latency rather than reducing it.

options.text: Apply the control-driven filter as early as possible in the query path to reduce the rows the warehouse must process
options.explanation: Applying a highly selective filter early in the query allows the warehouse to prune rows before any expensive joins or aggregations, minimizing the data volume processed and returning results faster.

options.text: Switch the control from a dropdown to a free-text box to avoid loading the list of values
options.explanation: Switching to free-text avoids the list-of-values query but may worsen overall usability and query performance if users enter imprecise values.

questions.stem: What should be rotated periodically for connections that use service-account credentials?

options.text: The workbook version tags associated with the connection
options.explanation: Version tags are metadata on workbook history and have no connection to credential security.

options.text: The user attributes attached to the service account in Sigma
options.explanation: User attributes drive row-level filtering and personalization; rotating them would change the data each user sees, not improve credential security.

options.text: The materialization schedules linked to datasets using that connection
options.explanation: Materialization schedules control when cached results are refreshed; they are separate from the authentication credentials used by the connection.

options.text: The credentials or secrets for the service account to reduce the exposure window of any compromised key
options.explanation: Rotating service-account credentials (passwords, API keys, or OAuth secrets) on a regular schedule limits the damage if credentials are compromised — an attacker who obtains an old credential cannot use it after rotation.

questions.stem: Which function family is most appropriate for replacing nulls with fallback values?

options.text: Window functions such as running sum or lag
options.explanation: Window functions like running sum or lag operate over ordered sets of rows to compute positional or cumulative values; they are not designed for null replacement.

options.text: Lookup functions that retrieve values from another element
options.explanation: Lookup functions retrieve matching values from another element based on a key; they do not test for null or substitute fallback values.

options.text: Conditional functions such as `Coalesce` or `IsNull`
options.explanation: `Coalesce` returns the first non-null value from a list of expressions, and `IsNull` checks whether a value is null — both are specifically designed for null-handling logic, such as replacing null with a default value, a zero, or a string like 'Unknown'.

options.text: Date functions such as `DateAdd` or `DateTrunc`
options.explanation: Date functions manipulate date and time expressions; they are not used for null replacement in general-purpose columns.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about embedding Sigma are accurate? (Select TWO)

options.text: Signed JWTs or embed secrets can authorize embedded access without requiring the end user to have a Sigma account
options.explanation: Sigma embedding uses signed credentials (embed client ID and secret) to authorize iframe access, allowing organizations to embed analytics for users who are not Sigma-licensed accounts.

options.text: Embed parameters can pass dynamic context such as tenant filters and user attributes into the embedded workbook
options.explanation: The embed URL can include signed parameters that map to user attributes, control values, or filter overrides, enabling dynamic personalization and tenant isolation per viewer.

options.text: Every workbook must be fully materialized before it can be embedded
options.explanation: Materialization is an optional performance optimization; it is not a prerequisite for embedding. Workbooks query the warehouse live when embedded.

options.text: Embedding a workbook removes the need to configure any permission or security settings for the embedded content
options.explanation: Embedded workbooks still inherit the security configuration set in Sigma — user attributes, workspace permissions, and dataset-level filters all apply during embedded sessions.

questions.stem: Which TWO practices most improve governed reuse in Sigma? (Select TWO)

options.text: Publish shared datasets that centralize join logic and metric definitions
options.explanation: Shared published datasets ensure all workbooks reference the same join logic, table definitions, and governance rules, reducing divergence across teams.

options.text: Rebuild all joins independently in every workbook so each team controls its own definitions
options.explanation: Rebuilding joins independently in each workbook increases the risk of drift — teams may implement the same join with subtle differences — and creates a maintenance burden when source schemas change.

options.text: Centralize common calculations and column aliases in reusable dataset assets
options.explanation: Defining common calculations, renamed columns, and standardized metrics in a dataset means those definitions are available to all workbooks that use it, avoiding duplication and inconsistency.

options.text: Grant every user full Admin rights so they can access any dataset they need
options.explanation: Granting full Admin rights to all users violates the principle of least privilege and introduces security and governance risks unrelated to dataset reuse.

questions.stem: Which TWO controls are most relevant when securing multi-tenant analytics in Sigma? (Select TWO)

options.text: User attributes mapped to tenant identifiers for dynamic row-level filtering
options.explanation: User attributes enable dynamic row-level security — each embedded or logged-in user automatically sees only the rows matching their tenant, enforced at query time by Sigma.

options.text: Granular workspace or workbook permissions that control which teams can access each asset
options.explanation: Workspace and workbook permissions control which users and teams can view, edit, or manage each content asset, preventing one tenant's users from accessing another tenant's workbooks.

options.text: Workbook color themes applied per tenant for visual differentiation
options.explanation: Color themes are cosmetic customizations; they do not enforce data isolation between tenants.

options.text: Scheduled materialization runs that pre-cache data per tenant each night
options.explanation: Materialization schedules optimize query performance; they do not provide any security boundary between tenants, as all tenants would read from the same cached table unless additional filtering is applied.

questions.stem: Which TWO steps are most likely to improve a slow Sigma workbook? (Select TWO)

options.text: Apply selective filters before large joins to reduce the rows processed by the warehouse
options.explanation: Pushing selective filters early in the query path reduces the number of rows the warehouse joins and aggregates, which is the most impactful type of SQL optimization for large datasets.

options.text: Schedule materialization for expensive repeated query results
options.explanation: Materializing expensive intermediate results as warehouse tables means repeated workbook loads read pre-computed data rather than re-executing costly calculations each time.

options.text: Disable all workbook controls to prevent users from triggering additional queries
options.explanation: Disabling controls removes useful interactivity without addressing the root cause of poor performance — the underlying query structure or data volume.

options.text: Duplicate the workbook into multiple folders so queries run in parallel on separate copies
options.explanation: Duplicating a workbook into multiple folders results in the same SQL being executed independently on each copy; it does not reduce individual query cost and adds maintenance overhead.

questions.stem: Which TWO capabilities make input tables useful for planning workflows? (Select TWO)

options.text: Business users can manually enter values such as budgets or targets directly in the workbook
options.explanation: Input tables surface a spreadsheet-like data entry experience inside the workbook, allowing business users to enter planning values — budgets, targets, adjustments, annotations — without leaving the Sigma environment.

options.text: Entered values are written back to the connected warehouse for persistence and downstream use
options.explanation: The entered values are written back to a warehouse table, making them available as a persistent data source that can be joined against actuals or referenced by other workbooks in downstream analysis.

options.text: Input tables automatically generate signed embed tokens for the data they contain
options.explanation: Signed embed tokens are generated by the embedding application server using the Sigma embed secret; input tables are not involved in token generation.

options.text: Input tables delete version history entries as new values are entered
options.explanation: Input tables do not delete version history; Sigma retains a history of workbook changes independently of input table operations.

questions.stem: Which TWO workbook design choices best support a polished analyst experience? (Select TWO)

options.text: Configure controls to target relevant elements so filters apply meaningfully and intuitively
options.explanation: Controls configured with appropriate targets make workbooks intuitive — users understand which filters affect which charts, reducing confusion and supporting self-service analysis.

options.text: Organize related content across clearly named pages with consistent navigation
options.explanation: Organizing content across well-named pages with logical navigation allows analysts to find what they need quickly and understand the workbook's analytical flow.

options.text: Permanently hide all controls even when users need to filter the data
options.explanation: Permanently hiding controls that users need to filter data frustrates analysts and defeats the self-service purpose of interactive workbooks.

options.text: Avoid published datasets so each page builds independently from raw warehouse tables
options.explanation: Avoiding published datasets means each page must independently rebuild joins and metric logic, increasing maintenance burden, inconsistency risk, and query complexity.

questions.stem: Why is granular sharing generally safer than granting broad workspace access to everyone?

options.text: Broad workspace access always triggers additional warehouse compute, increasing cost
options.explanation: Access permissions govern who can view content, not the warehouse compute cost of running queries; broader permissions do not inherently increase cost.

options.text: Granular sharing limits access to only the content each user actually needs, following the principle of least privilege
options.explanation: Least-privilege access control — giving users access only to the specific assets they need — reduces the blast radius if an account is compromised and prevents accidental exposure of sensitive analytical content.

options.text: Granular sharing automatically replaces row-level filtering for all workbooks in the workspace
options.explanation: Sharing permissions control who can open a workbook; they do not automatically configure row-level filtering within it. Row-level security requires user attributes and dataset filters.

options.text: Broad workspace access disables version history for all workbooks in the workspace
options.explanation: Version history is a workbook feature that persists regardless of the sharing model applied to the workspace.

questions.stem: Which Sigma capability is most useful when an application must export workbook results programmatically on a schedule?

options.text: Workbook version tags that snapshot the workbook definition at export time
options.explanation: Version tags mark workbook definition milestones; they do not trigger or automate data exports.

options.text: User attributes that restrict which rows are included in the exported data
options.explanation: User attributes control data visibility at query time; they are a security mechanism, not an export automation tool.

options.text: The Sigma REST API, which supports programmatic scheduled exports of workbook content
options.explanation: The Sigma REST API exposes export endpoints that allow an external application or scheduler to trigger workbook exports programmatically, in formats such as CSV, XLSX, or PDF, on a custom schedule.

options.text: Materialization schedules that pre-cache the data and make it available for download
options.explanation: Materialization pre-caches query results as warehouse tables; the cached data is not automatically pushed or exported to an external destination.

questions.stem: Why should a Sigma developer avoid unnecessary cross-joins in workbook modeling?

options.text: Cross-joins are not supported in Sigma's visual join builder and must be written in raw SQL
options.explanation: Sigma's visual join builder supports multiple join types; while cross-joins may require specific configuration, they are not entirely unsupported in Sigma.

options.text: Cross-joins prevent materialization from being scheduled on the affected workbook
options.explanation: The presence of a cross-join does not prevent materialization from being configured; materialization can persist any query result, including expensive ones.

options.text: Cross-joins disable workbook controls from filtering the joined elements
options.explanation: Workbook controls and filters operate on the output of the query regardless of join type; cross-joins do not disable control filtering.

options.text: Cross-joins multiply row counts between tables, producing extremely large intermediate result sets and expensive warehouse queries
options.explanation: A cross-join produces the Cartesian product of two tables — a table with M rows joined to one with N rows produces M × N output rows. For large tables this creates massive intermediate result sets that consume significant warehouse compute, memory, and cost, often unintentionally.
